narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 64% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your bottom-decile rankings in both your first- and third-year school grades—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position—along with your LSAT score of 33, undergraduate GPA of 3.0, middle family income bracket, Asian racial background, male gender, and plan to attend full-time. These details paint a picture of solid but underperforming academic metrics compared to the average applicant, who tends to rank around the fifth or sixth decile in school, scores about 36.7 on the LSAT, and holds a 3.21 GPA. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your third-year class rank, which had the biggest pull toward predicting failure since being in the lowest decile signals higher risk than average. Right behind that was your LSAT score, as its below-average level further nudged the prediction toward failure rather than success. Your first-year rank played a similar supporting role, reinforcing concerns about consistent underperformance. Family income in the middle bracket contributed next, followed by your Asian background, which together amplified the risk signals in the model's eyes. Your slightly lower undergraduate GPA added to this, while your gender and full-time status had the least sway but still tilted slightly toward the unfavorable outcome. In essence, no single factor sealed the decision, but the top influences—especially those low class rankings and LSAT—stood out as the heaviest weights pushing away from a pass prediction. Compared to typical students who pass, your metrics cluster below the norms that usually predict success, creating this cumulative concern. Looking ahead, you could realistically retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 37, which might strengthen future applications by countering that key negative influence. Similarly, building a stronger academic record through additional coursework or demonstrated improvement in later grades could help shift perceptions of your class performance. With focused preparation, many students in similar spots turn things around for the bar itself—consider targeted tutoring or study strategies proven for law exams. We're rooting for your next steps.
